prompt:
Build keyed_recon.ts — the keyed reconciler for our teaching renderer: it diffs two element trees into a flat list of patch ops. No React import, no JSX; plain TypeScript that runs under node's type stripping (annotations only, keep explicit ./file.ts import specifiers).

Tree shape (all fields always present): { type: string; key: string | null; props: Record<string, string | number | boolean>; children: Element[] }.

Export diff(oldRoot, newRoot) returning an array of ops. Roots must have the same type — otherwise throw Error(`root type changed: expected ${oldType}, got ${newType}`). The root's own key is ignored (keys only mean something between siblings).

The four op shapes, exactly:
- { op: "update", path, set, remove } — path is the list of child indices from the root in the NEW tree ([] is the root). set holds props that are new or changed (compare values with Object.is). remove holds prop names present in old but not in new, sorted lexicographically. Emit the op only when there is something in set or remove, but always include both fields.
- { op: "insert", parent, index, node } — parent is a path as above; index is the position in the NEW children list; node is the entire new subtree as given (no ops are emitted inside an inserted subtree).
- { op: "remove", parent, index } — index is the position in the OLD children list.
- { op: "move", parent, from, to } — from is the old index, to is the new index.

Matching children within a parent: a keyed new child matches the old child with the same key AND the same type — same key but different type is a remove plus an insert, never a move. Keys must be unique within a single children list (check both the old and the new list); on a duplicate throw Error(`duplicate key: ${key}`). Unkeyed children pair positionally: the i-th unkeyed new child pairs with the i-th unkeyed old child, and the pair only matches if the types agree (a type mismatch is remove + insert, and it still consumes that positional pairing). Matched pairs recurse; their descendants are addressed through NEW-tree paths.

Op order is pinned. For each matched pair, in pre-order over the NEW tree: first the node's own update op (if any); then its child-list ops — every remove in DESCENDING old-index order, then a single left-to-right walk of the new children emitting insert for unmatched new children and move for matched children that do not hold their place; then recurse into the matched pairs in new-list order.

Minimal moves. The children that stay put (no move op) must form a longest strictly increasing subsequence of old indices taken in new order — so a keyed reorder emits the fewest possible moves (moving the last child to the front is ONE move, not three). When several longest increasing subsequences exist, keep the one that prefers the earliest new positions: scanning matched children in new order, keep a child exactly when a maximum-length subsequence can still be completed through it — i.e. the kept set of new positions is lexicographically smallest among the maximum-length options.

Acceptance: `node --test test_keyed_recon.ts` fully green. The test file is read-only and pins exact op lists — order, field names, indices, error messages.
notes: react-internals build seed: NO vendored React, no JSX — plain .ts under node type stripping, node --test. keyed_recon.ts is absent in the baseline (module-not-found is the pre-fix failure). Reconciler semantics: keyed match requires key+type, unkeyed pair positionally, removes emitted descending then one new-order walk (insert/move), pre-order recursion with new-tree paths, LIS-based minimal moves with an earliest-new-position tie-break so every fixture has a unique exact op list (the move-last-to-front fixture distinguishes LIS reconcilers from React's lastPlacedIndex heuristic, which would emit three moves).
